sentence: Subjective:
The patient, Brittany Edwards, a 32-year-old female, presents with irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) and functional abdominal pain.
She reports experiencing flares of abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting.
She was previously diagnosed with IBS and treated with Bentyl.
She was also prescribed Reglan, but instructed to stop using it and use Zofran as needed for nausea.
She reports eating spicy foods, including spicy shrimp, and has noticed an increase in diarrhea and abdominal pain after consuming these foods.
She also reports drinking tequila or dark liquor when eating out and occasionally having a martini.
She has not been using the prescribed fiber supplement, Cirtucel, regularly.
Past medical history:
The patient has a history of IBS, treated with Bentyl and Reglan.
She was previously instructed to start a low-dose fiber supplement, probiotics, and Bentyl for intestinal cramping.
Review of systems:
The patient reports experiencing diarrhea, nausea, and abdominal pain.
She reports having cramping and sharp pains in her lower abdomen.
Current medications:
Bentyl 20 milligrams as needed 30 to 45 minutes prior to eating out; Zofran as needed for nausea; Reglan was previously prescribed but stopped; Cirtucel was prescribed but not used regularly.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient's abdomen is soft, non-tender, with no masses or organomegaly.
Normal active bowel sounds.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has IBS with functional abdominal pain, aggravated by high stress, poor eating habits, and poor sleeping habits.
Problem list: IBS; IBS with functional abdominal pain; Active; IBS aggravated by high stress, poor eating habits, and poor sleeping habits; Active.
Plan:
I prescribed Cirtucel twice daily for regularity.
I instructed the patient to use Bentyl 20 milligrams 30 to 45 minutes prior to eating out at a restaurant and to take one tablet before eating and one tablet later.
I also instructed the patient to use Zofran as needed for nausea, but to reduce greasy and fatty foods and alcohol consumption.
I prescribed Elavil 25 milligrams at bedtime for IBS.
I asked the patient to follow up in one month by emailing me regarding any side effects and issues with Elavil or if adjustments are needed.
Follow-up:
The patient will follow up with me in one month by emailing me regarding any side effects and issues with Elavil or if adjustments are needed.
